html:
RECOUP Integrative, Root-Cause Osteoporosis and Metabolic Bone Disease Program

Conditions treated

  1. Osteoporosis and osteopenia are bone diseases that develop when bone mineral density and bone mass decrease or when the structure and strength of bone change.
  2. Hyperparathyroidism is a condition that occurs when the parathyroid glands produce too much parathyroid hormone (PTH), which can lead to high blood calcium levels.
  3. Vitamin D deficiency: Rickets (children) and osteomalacia (adults) affect bone development and cause bone pain, poor growth, and soft, weak bones that can lead to bone deformities.
  4. Paget disease of bone is a chronic autoimmune inflammatory disease that disrupts the replacement of old bone tissue with new bone tissue. Over time, affected bones may become fragile and misshapen.
  5. Osteogenesis Imperfecta - is a genetic or heritable disease in which bones fracture easily, often with no apparent cause or minimal injury.
  6. X-linked Hypophosphatemia - low levels of phosphate in the blood.
  7. Hypophosphatasia - low level of alkaline phosphatase in the body.
  8. Renal bone disease (renal osteodystrophy) - is a bone disease that can occur in people with chronic kidney disease.

Facilities available:

Assessment

  • Interdisciplinary evaluation using RECOUP's proprietary Digital Tools and consultation with a group of specialists
  • RECOUP Entire Person Health Score and Care Plan Generation using AI: EPHS-S, EPHS-P, EPHS-O
  • Virtual Ergonomic Workplace Analysis Tool
  • Lifestyle medicine assessment
  • Diagnostic testing: Sunlight bone mineral densitometry test: definitive for children, screening test for adults
  • Special blood, saliva, stool, and urine testing
  • Risk stratification: FRAX scoring for India
  • Assessment of fall risk
  • Instrumented posture analysis
  • Instrumented gait analysis
  • Instrumented foot pressure scans

Education and Prevention

  • Patient Education
  • Bone health program for children and adolescents
  • Strategies for Fall and Fracture Prevention
  • Nutrition and Culinary Education
  • Personalized exercise prescriptions
  • Smoking cessation program
  • Alcohol cessation program
  • Weight management program
  • Ergonomic workplace analysis (virtual or in-person)
  • Home fall risk assessment and modifications

Treatment

  • Physical Rehabilitation: pain management, specialized strengthening and balance training program
  • Whole-body vibration therapy
  • Virtual and augmented reality-based therapy
  • Aquatic therapy
  • Tai Chi
  • Dynamic Neurocognitive Imagery (Franklin Method)
  • Nutrition Assessment and Interventions.
  • Dietary Supplements.
  • Footwear modification, customized insoles and orthotics.
  • Health and wellness coaching
  • Lifestyle management includes sleep, stress, physical activity, and positive relationships.
  • Assessment and treatment of root causes, such as infection, heavy metal toxicity, microbial imbalance, HPA axis dysfunction, hormonal dysregulation, and inflammation.
  • Psychologically informed rehabilitation, e.g., Emotional Awareness and Expression Therapy, Pain Reprocessing Therapy, Acceptance and Commitment Therapy, Progressive Goal Attainment Program.
  • Medical management: Administration of osteoporosis therapies as a daycare procedure (IV bisphosphonates, subcutaneous Denosumab, Romosozmab, Teriparatide, Abaloparatide)
  • In-patient facilities for treatment and rehabilitation

RECOUP'S Interdisciplinary Team

  • Lead Physician (Dr Deepak Sharan)
  • Orthopedic Surgeon
  • Rehabilitation Physicians
  • Regenerative Medicine Physicians
  • Occupational Health Physician
  • Orthopedic Rheumatologists
  • Functional and Lifestyle Medicine Physicians
  • Ayurveda Physician
  • Naturopathy Physicians
  • Physiotherapists
  • Occupational Therapists
  • Ergonomists
  • Aquatic Therapists
  • Yoga Therapists
  • Tai Chi and Qi Gong Therapists
  • Alexander and Feldenkrais Teachers
  • Clinical Psychologists
  • Nutritionists
  • Health Coaches
  • Nurses
  • Administrative Managers

Who should attend this program?

  • Known diagnosis of osteopenia, osteoporosis or metabolic bone disease
  • Sustained a fragility fracture (low-energy or without significant trauma)
  • Has not had appropriate screening

Why RECOUP?

  • 33 years of experience treating Osteoporosis and Metabolic Bone Disease
  • Global recognition for osteoporosis research: Dr Sharan reported that 33.5% of Indian IT Professionals aged between 20 and 40 years had osteopenia, and 11.6% had osteoporosis (Sharan D et al. Low Bone Mineral Density in young Indian IT Professionals: Prevalence and Risk factors. 30th International Congress on Occupational Health, 2012 in Cancun, Mexico).
  • A referral or prescription is not required.
  • We would happily coordinate with your existing primary care provider to ensure that treatments align with your overall health goals.

What is osteoporosis?

Osteopenia and osteoporosis are conditions characterized by the loss of bone density and strength, which increases an individual's risk of sustaining a fracture.

How is osteoporosis diagnosed?

The diagnosis of osteoporosis is established if a person has a T score less than or equal to - 2.5 on a DEXA scan or if a fragility fracture has occurred. A fragility fracture results from stress that would not usually cause a fracture, such as those sustained during falls from a standing height. Osteopenia is a T score between -1.0 and -2.5 on the DEXA scan.

What are the symptoms of osteoporosis?

Osteoporosis often does not cause symptoms, so routine screening is necessary. The only symptom associated with osteoporosis is the pain if a fracture occurs.

Who gets osteoporosis?

Although more common in women, men are also frequently affected. There are numerous risk factors for osteoporosis, including being post-menopausal, having a family history of osteoporosis or fragility fracture, age, thin stature, ethnicity, use of various medications (including steroids, many seizure and antacid medications, and some treatments used in the management of breast and prostate cancer), drinking alcohol in excess, tobacco use, celiac disease, vitamin D deficiency, hyperthyroidism (including over treated hypothyroidism), inflammatory arthritis and many others.

When should I have my bone density tested?

ISCD currently recommends testing for various groups, including the following:

  • Women aged 65 and over
  • Post-menopausal women younger than age 65 with certain risk factors*
  • Women during the menopausal transition with certain risk factors*
  • Men aged 70 and over
  • Men younger than age 70 with certain risk factors*
  • Any adult with a history of fragility fracture or risk factors for low bone mass

* Risk factors include low body weight, prior fracture, high-risk medication use, and certain medical conditions

It is ideal to have a DEXA scan around ages 30-35 as a baseline and then another one a year after menopause to compare the degree of bone loss.
